Two-dimensional echocardiography and coronary angiography are routine examinations to evaluate the coronary lesions; however, these are not adequate to assess the wall morphology of the coronary artery (CA). Intravascular ultrasound imaging (IVUS), a new technology for the evaluation of the coronary artery lumen and wall morphology in vivo, was performed for patients after KD in their long-term. Intravascular ultrasonography has been increasingly used to assess vascular pathologies due its high resolution imaging capabilities that allow visualization beyond what is possible with conventional echocardiographic techniques. After Kawasaki disease (KD), intravenous ultrasound was conducted on the coronary arteries of children in order to evaluate both wall morphology and lumen. texts.full_text: getAttribute() not working with Sizzle in IE 11 I have some Javascript code that works fine on every other browser but (of course) IE. Could someone tell me if there is a problem with my code, and if there is not, suggest a fix? Note: IE returns null for href attribute. for(var a=Sizzle("*"),i=0;i<a.leNgth;i++) { a[i].onclick=function(){ window.open(this.getAttribute("href"),"_self"); } } AThe issue you're encountering is due to the way Internet Explorer handles attributes compared to other browsers. In IE, to access an attribute of an HTML element, you should use the property directly instead of using `getAttribute()` method. Here's how your code could be modified to work in IE 11: ```javascript for(var a=Sizzle("*"),i=0;i<a.length;i++) { a[i].onclick=function(){ window.open(this.href, "_self"); } } ``` By directly accessing `this.href`, the code will function as expected in Internet Explorer. This is because `href` is treated as a property of the element, whereas other attributes would need to be accessed via the `getAttribute()` method. Remember that this solution works specifically for the "href" attribute. For other attributes, you may still need to use `getAttribute()`. However, for the "href" attribute, using `this.href` is both simpler and more compatible with different browsers.

The new algorithm has been to Google users in the US, UK, India, Australia, and Canada since February 5, 2011, and Google plans to roll it out to the rest of the world in the coming months. The Caffeine algorithm uses Google's Web cache to deliver fresh search results. Google's cache of the web is updated in real-time, and the search engine uses the cache to deliver search results to users. This is the first major change to Google's web search algorithm in five years. The Caffeine algorithm is Google's second search algorithm, the first being Google's proprietary search algorithm, PageRank. texts.full_text: quency, and high temperature applications. Signal amplification devices in radar, cellular, and satellite communications frequently use GaAs based HEMTs. GaAs semiconductor materials have higher electron mobility and a lower source resistance than Si, which allows them to operate at higher frequencies. GaAs has, however, a relatively small bandgap preventing the use of GaAs HEMTs in high power at high frequency applications. Improvements in the manufacturing of gallium nitride (GaN) semi-conductor materials and semi-conductor materials made from an alloy consisting of aluminum nitride and gallium nitride (AlGaN) have focused interest on the use of AlGaN/GaN HEMTs for use in high frequency, high power and high temperature applications. AlGaN and GaN have large bandgaps making them superior to Si and GaAs for these types of applications. The use of AlGaN layered upon GaN and the mismatch of the crystal structures of the two materials and their different bandgap energies results in the creation of a two-dimensional electron gas (2DEG) under certain circumstances. The 2DEG layer accumulates in the smaller bandgap material and contains a very high electron concentration. But also through action—leaving behind all for Christ’s sake? McManus encourages this contemplation, urging us toward authenticity with ourselves before seeking fulfillment from external sources. texts.full_text: and Leigh Sports Village are part of broader strategies to provide versatile venues that can support multiple sports while also serving community needs. Regarding Brentford's stadium project: 1. Irish ownership: There was indeed speculation about Irish ownership, but as far as I'm concerned, no definitive agreement has been reached. 2. Simplified Stadium Design and Capacity Reductions: - The idea is to improve the construction process by increasing capacity (potentially from 30k+ down) which can lead to quicker construction times and higher costs while still being suitable for Championship matches if Brentford qualifies in future seasons. This approach aims at making financial sense given that being a Premier League club remains uncertain. The reduced size also means avoiding competition with smaller, more established clubs nearby. 3. Non-Matchday Revenue: By relying on essential facilities and facilities rather than extensive corporate entertainment options (like bars or pubs), Brentford hopes to avoid high operational costs in an already saturated market. 4. Tenancy vs Ownership: - even if Irish does join the project, it's unlikely that they will operate as tenants/renters of a stadium owned by the entity rather than outright tenants. However, your concern about planning permission and game time regulations (like prohibiting Friday/Sunday matches) is intriguing but seems more specific to specific local contexts. The Notting Hill & Luton City Cricket Association ("The Wanderers") has indeed faced problems with their proposed new ground in relation to such issues when considering hosting teams like Leinster RFC or London Irish Rugby. In summary, the discussions over stadium ownership and design reflect a broader trend towards more sustainable models that balance economic viability with community benefits.

texts.full_text: is a method for tying wiring harnesses and cable looms, traditionally used in telecommunication, naval, and aerospace applications. This old cable management technique, taught to generations of linemen, is still used in some modern applications since it does not create obstructions along the length of the cable, avoiding the handling problems of cables groomed by plastic or hook-and-loop cable ties. Cable lacing uses a thin cord, traditionally made of waxed linen, to bind together a group of cables using a series of running lockstitches. Flat lacing tapes made of modern materials such as nylon, polyester, Teflon, fiberglass, and Nomex are also available with a variety of coatings to improve knot holding. Styles The lacing begins and ends with a whipping or other knot to secure the free ends. Wraps are spaced relative to the overall harness diameter to maintain the wiring in a tight, neat bundle, and the ends are then neatly trimmed. In addition to continuous or running lacing, there are a variety of lacing patterns used in different circumstances. In some cases stand-alone knoCable Loom A cable loom is an electrical power assembly consisting of wire conductors, usually with many terminations on each end and bundled into cables by using tapes (a.k.a., “cotton braid”). A special type of lacing tape called airloom or hook-and-lace tape has loops that are used to cinch down the lace. The loom is then protected from abrasion, fluids, heat etc. with an outer covering. A cable harness assembly typically consists of several components and subassemblies including: Conductor Electrical connectors Cable Looms (a.k.a., “cotton braids”) Lacing cord or tape (e.g., airloom tape) Thermal protection devices such as sleeves, tapes etc. Braided sleeving to provide abrasion resistance.
